title: Conditional Probability & Bayes
subtitle: Updating beliefs when new information arrives
intro: Conditional probability answers a question you already ask intuitively: once you know something happened, how should your odds change? Formally, P(A|B) is the probability of A among outcomes where B is true. The sample space shrinks to B, and probabilities are renormalized so they still sum to 1.

The law of total probability breaks an event into pieces across a partition (disjoint scenarios that cover everything). Bayes' theorem reverses conditioning: it turns hard-to-measure quantities like P(hypothesis|data) into products of easier pieces like P(data|hypothesis) and prior odds. Together, these tools are the backbone of probabilistic reasoning under uncertainty.

Quant interviews love this material because it tests whether you update beliefs correctly instead of chasing shortcuts. Disease testing, urn problems, trading signals, and card-drawing puzzles all reduce to the same pattern: restrict the sample space, apply total probability for the denominator, then use Bayes to flip the conditioning direction.

Bayes' theorem is the interview litmus test for whether you update beliefs correctly when new evidence arrives. The Stat 110 pattern—partition the world, compute a total-probability denominator, then flip the conditioning direction—shows up in disease testing, manufacturing defects, and trading signals. Green Book-style puzzles often hide the partition in plain sight; your job is to write P(hypothesis | data) before reaching for a calculator.

Bayes' theorem is the interview litmus test for whether you update beliefs correctly when new evidence arrives. The Stat 110 pattern—partition the world, compute a total-probability denominator, then flip the conditioning direction—shows up in disease testing, manufacturing defects, and trading signals. Green Book-style puzzles often hide the partition in plain sight; your job is to write P(hypothesis | data) before reaching for a calculator.

questions.question: Events A and B satisfy P(B) > 0. Which formula correctly defines the conditional probability P(A|B)?

explanations.A: Dividing P(A) by P(B) has no probabilistic meaning here; conditioning requires the overlap with B in the numerator.
explanations.B: Correct. Among outcomes where B occurs, the fraction that also satisfies A is P(A ∩ B) / P(B).
explanations.C: P(A ∩ B) / P(A) equals P(B|A), the probability of B given A — the conditioning direction is reversed.
explanations.D: Probabilities are never defined by subtraction of unrelated events; this can even be negative.

remediation.question: In a restricted sample space where B has already occurred, P(A|B) counts outcomes in both A and B divided by outcomes in:

explanations.A: Conditioning on B means B is the new denominator, not A.
explanations.B: Correct. We renormalize within B, so favorable outcomes are A ∩ B and the total is B.
explanations.C: The original sample space is too large once we know B occurred.
explanations.D: The complement of A is unrelated to the definition of P(A|B).

remediation.question: If P(A ∩ B) = 0.12 and P(B) = 0.4, then P(A|B) equals:

explanations.A: 0.12 is P(A ∩ B) before renormalizing within B.
explanations.C: 0.48 would multiply the probabilities; conditioning requires division by P(B).
explanations.D: 0.028 comes from multiplying 0.12 × 0.4, which is not the conditional formula.

questions.question: From a standard 52-card deck you draw one card and are told it is a heart. What is P(ace | heart)?

explanations.A: 1/52 is the unconditional probability of drawing the ace of hearts from the full deck.
explanations.B: Correct. Given a heart, 13 equally likely hearts remain; exactly 1 is an ace, so P = 1/13.
explanations.C: 1/4 is P(heart) unconditionally, not the probability of an ace among hearts.
explanations.D: 4/52 counts all aces in the deck, but conditioning restricts us to the 13 hearts.

remediation.question: Given that a drawn card is red (26 cards), what is P(heart | red)?

explanations.A: Correct. Half of the 26 red cards are hearts: 13/26 = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/4 is the unconditional probability of drawing any heart.
explanations.C: 13/52 simplifies to 1/4 and ignores the conditioning on red.
explanations.D: 1/26 would be one heart among 26 red cards counted incorrectly.

remediation.question: A bag has 3 red and 2 blue marbles. One red marble is drawn. Given this, P(red on next draw without replacement) is:

explanations.A: 3/5 is the probability before any marble was removed.
explanations.B: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2 and is correct arithmetic, but 1/2 is the cleaner answer choice.
explanations.C: Correct. After removing one red, 2 red remain out of 4 total: 2/4 = 1/2.
explanations.D: 2/5 would be P(blue), not P(red), after one red is removed.

questions.question: It rains on 30% of days and is cloudy on 50% of days. On rainy days, 90% are cloudy, so P(cloudy | rain) = 0.9. Which quantity is generally NOT equal to P(rain | cloudy)?

explanations.A: Correct. P(cloudy | rain) = 0.9, but P(rain | cloudy) = P(rain ∩ cloudy)/P(cloudy) is typically much smaller because rain is rarer than cloudiness.
explanations.B: This fraction is exactly the definition of P(rain | cloudy).
explanations.C: This is Bayes' theorem applied correctly to compute P(rain | cloudy).
explanations.D: 0.9 equals P(cloudy | rain) by statement, not P(rain | cloudy).

remediation.question: P(A|B) and P(B|A) are equal only in special cases. In general, reversing the conditioning:

explanations.A: Correct. The denominators P(B) and P(A) differ, so the ratios usually differ.
explanations.B: Equality holds only when P(A) = P(B) and the overlap is symmetric — not in general.
explanations.C: There is no reason reversed conditionals should center at 0.5.
explanations.D: Both are defined whenever the conditioning event has positive probability.

remediation.question: A medical test has P(positive | disease) = 0.99. The quantity P(disease | positive) is:

explanations.A: Correct. A high sensitivity does not imply a high posterior when the disease is rare.
explanations.B: Only in unrealistic cases (very high base rate) would the posterior near 0.99.
explanations.C: The posterior depends on the base rate and false-positive rate, not automatically 0.5.
explanations.D: A positive test never guarantees disease unless the test is perfect and disease-free cases cannot test positive.

questions.question: Events B₁, B₂, …, Bₙ form a partition of the sample space (mutually exclusive and exhaustive). Which identity is the law of total probability?

explanations.A: Correct. A happens either with B₁ or B₂ or …; weight each conditional piece by P(Bᵢ).
explanations.B: This mixes conditioning direction and repeats P(A) incorrectly.
explanations.C: Summing conditionals without weights P(Bᵢ) ignores how likely each scenario is.
explanations.D: Multiplying P(A) by P(B₁) is not a valid decomposition.

remediation.question: A partition means the Bᵢ are disjoint and their union is the entire sample space. Therefore Σ P(Bᵢ) equals:

explanations.A: Probabilities of a partition cannot all sum to zero.
explanations.B: Correct. Exhaustive disjoint events account for all outcomes, so probabilities sum to 1.
explanations.C: P(A) is unrelated to whether Bᵢ form a partition.
explanations.D: The sum of partition probabilities is always 1, regardless of A.

remediation.question: A coin is equally likely to come from Box H (P heads = 0.8) or Box T (P heads = 0.3). Before seeing a flip, P(heads) equals:

explanations.A: 0.8 is P(heads | Box H) only, ignoring that Box T is equally likely.
explanations.B: 0.3 is P(heads | Box T) only.
explanations.C: Correct. (0.5)(0.8) + (0.5)(0.3) = 0.55 by total probability.
explanations.D: Probabilities cannot exceed 1; this sum would be invalid.

questions.question: A factory has Machine A (40% of output, 2% defective) and Machine B (60% of output, 5% defective). What is P(random item is defective)?

explanations.A: 0.02 is the defect rate from Machine A alone, not the plant-wide rate.
explanations.B: 0.05 is the defect rate from Machine B alone.
explanations.D: 0.07 adds the rates without weighting by production share.

remediation.question: Urn I: 3 red, 1 blue. Urn II: 1 red, 3 blue. You pick an urn uniformly at random, then draw one ball. P(red) equals:

explanations.B: 1/4 is P(red | Urn II) only.
explanations.C: 3/4 is P(red | Urn I) only.
explanations.D: 2/8 counts favorable outcomes but ignores the random urn choice.

remediation.question: Scenario X occurs with probability 0.3 and gives P(A) = 0.6; scenario Y occurs with probability 0.7 and gives P(A) = 0.2. Then P(A) equals:

explanations.B: 0.4 might come from averaging 0.6 and 0.2 without weights.
explanations.C: 0.8 exceeds any single conditional probability and cannot be the total.
explanations.D: 0.18 is only the contribution from scenario X.

questions.question: Bayes' theorem states P(H | E) = [P(E | H) P(H)] / P(E). If P(E) is unknown but H and Hᶜ partition the space, you should compute P(E) as:
options: P(E | H) P(H) + P(E | Hᶜ) P(Hᶜ)

explanations.A: Adding conditionals without multiplying by prior weights ignores how likely H and Hᶜ are.
explanations.B: Correct. This is the law of total probability applied to compute the denominator P(E).
explanations.C: This rearranges Bayes' theorem but does not help when P(E) is unknown.
explanations.D: Dividing a likelihood by a prior is not a valid formula for P(E).

remediation.question: In Bayes' theorem, P(H | E) is proportional to:

explanations.A: Correct. Posterior ∝ likelihood × prior; divide by P(E) to normalize.
explanations.B: P(H | E) P(E) equals P(H ∩ E), not the posterior numerator alone.
explanations.C: Dividing P(E) by P(H) does not yield a posterior.
explanations.D: Subtraction of probabilities is not part of Bayes' theorem.

remediation.question: The prior P(H) in Bayes' theorem represents:
options: your probability for H before observing evidence E
the probability of evidence given H

explanations.A: Correct. The prior is your baseline belief before seeing data E.
explanations.B: P(E | H) is the likelihood, not the prior.
explanations.C: P(H is false) would be P(Hᶜ), not P(H).
explanations.D: Priors depend on context; 0.5 is not universal.

questions.question: A disease affects 1% of people. A test has sensitivity P(+ | disease) = 0.95 and specificity P(− | no disease) = 0.90 (so P(+ | no disease) = 0.10). If someone tests positive, P(disease | +) is closest to:

explanations.A: 0.95 is the sensitivity P(+ | disease), not the posterior P(disease | +).
explanations.B: 0.50 ignores the rare base rate and high false-positive rate among healthy people.
explanations.D: 0.01 is the prior P(disease) before seeing the test result.

remediation.question: When a disease is rare, even a fairly accurate test can yield a low P(disease | positive) because:
options: false positives among healthy people can outnumber true positives

explanations.A: Correct. Many healthy people × moderate false-positive rate can swamp the few true positives.
explanations.B: Sensitivity can be high; the issue is the small diseased population.
explanations.C: Specificity 0 would make the test useless, but the problem assumes 0.90 specificity.
explanations.D: Priors are context-dependent, not forced to 0.5.

remediation.question: In Bayes problems, the denominator P(E) is often found using:

explanations.A: Correct. Split E across hypotheses, sum P(E | hypothesis) P(hypothesis).
explanations.B: Regression is unrelated to this discrete Bayes calculation.
explanations.C: Complements help with single events but not directly for mixed hypotheses.
explanations.D: Multiplying priors has no role in computing P(E).

questions.question: A quant fund's model says P(stock up tomorrow | bullish signal) = 0.7 and P(stock up | bearish signal) = 0.4. Signals are bullish 60% of days. What is P(stock up tomorrow) before seeing today's signal?

explanations.A: 0.7 is P(up | bullish) only, not the unconditional daily probability.
explanations.B: 0.4 is P(up | bearish) only.
explanations.D: 0.55 might come from averaging 0.7 and 0.4 without correct weights.

remediation.question: Before observing data, the overall P(event) is best computed by:
options: weighting conditional probabilities by scenario probabilities

explanations.A: Correct. Total probability weights each scenario by how often it arises.
explanations.B: The maximum conditional ignores less likely but still relevant scenarios.
explanations.C: 0.5 is not a default for real models.
explanations.D: Products of conditionals are for independent chains, not mixtures.

remediation.question: After seeing a bullish signal in the setup above, P(up | bullish) equals:

explanations.A: 0.58 is the unconditional P(up) before conditioning on the signal.
explanations.B: Correct. The problem states P(up | bullish) = 0.7 directly.
explanations.C: 0.42 might confuse P(bullish) with a conditional probability.
explanations.D: 0.6 is P(bullish), not P(up | bullish).

questions.question: Three urns: Urn 1 has 5 red / 5 blue; Urn 2 has 8 red / 2 blue; Urn 3 has 1 red / 9 blue. An urn is chosen uniformly at random, then one ball is drawn and it is red. P(the ball came from Urn 2 | red) equals:

explanations.B: 4/9 comes from dividing 0.8 by 1.8 without applying the equal urn priors correctly.
explanations.C: 8/18 adds numerators without weighting each urn by its prior probability.
explanations.D: 2/3 ignores the evidence from Urns 1 and 3.

remediation.question: In a Bayes urn problem, the posterior P(Urn i | red) is proportional to:

explanations.A: Correct. Prior on urn times likelihood of red given that urn.
explanations.B: This equals P(Urn i ∩ red), not the unnormalized posterior weight.
explanations.C: The likelihood alone ignores that some urns are less likely a priori.
explanations.D: The prior alone ignores how diagnostic the red draw is.

remediation.question: Two urns: Urn A is P(red)=0.9, Urn B is P(red)=0.1, each urn equally likely. After drawing red, P(Urn A | red) is:

explanations.A: 0.9 is P(red | A), not the posterior on the urn.
explanations.B: 0.5 is the prior before seeing red; the evidence strongly favors Urn A.
explanations.C: The denominator must be total P(red) = 0.5, not 1.0.

questions.question: A plant runs Line 1 (70% of units, 1% defective) and Line 2 (30% of units, 10% defective). A randomly inspected unit is defective. P(it came from Line 2 | defective) is closest to:

explanations.A: 0.30 is P(Line 2) before seeing the defect, not the posterior.
explanations.B: 0.10 is the defect rate on Line 2 alone, not P(Line 2 | defective).
explanations.D: 0.50 ignores how much more often Line 2 produces defects despite smaller volume.

remediation.question: When a defect is observed, Bayes tells you to weight each source by:

explanations.A: Correct. Likelihood of the evidence on each source times how common that source is.
explanations.B: The prior alone ignores how diagnostic the defect is for each line.
explanations.C: P(defect) is the denominator, not the unnormalized weight for one source.
explanations.D: Complements of priors are not the Bayes numerator.

remediation.question: When evidence E supports hypothesis H, Bayes says the posterior P(H | E) is higher than P(H) whenever:

explanations.A: Correct. Evidence is more likely under H than under alternatives, so posterior rises.
explanations.B: A 0.5 prior is not required for updating upward.
explanations.C: Certain evidence P(E)=1 is not needed for a posterior increase.
explanations.D: P(H | E) and P(E | H) are generally different quantities.

questions.question: A factory runs Line A (60% of output, 2% defective), Line B (25%, 5% defective), and Line C (15%, 12% defective). A randomly inspected unit is defective. What is P(it came from Line C | defective)?

explanations.B: 3/10 is P(C) before seeing the defect, not the posterior.
explanations.C: 12/100 is the defect rate on Line C alone, not P(C | defective).
explanations.D: 15/100 is the prior share of Line C, ignoring how diagnostic a defect is.

remediation.question: In Bayes' theorem, the denominator P(B) in P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B) is often found by:
options: Law of total probability over a partition

explanations.A: Correct. Sum P(B|scenario)P(scenario) across disjoint scenarios that cover the sample space.
explanations.B: P(A)P(B) equals joint probability only under independence, not generally P(B).
explanations.C: The largest prior is irrelevant to the normalization constant.
explanations.D: The prior is essential; Bayes combines prior with likelihood.

remediation.question: Supplier S1 provides 70% of parts (1% defective); S2 provides 30% (4% defective). Given a defective part, P(S2 | defective) equals:

explanations.B: 4/100 is the defect rate for S2 alone, not the posterior probability.
explanations.C: 30/100 is the prior P(S2) before observing the defect.
explanations.D: 1/2 would require equal posterior weight on both suppliers, which the unequal priors and rates do not produce.

questions.question: Tomorrow's weather: P(rain) = 0.30. The forecast says 'rain' with P(forecast rain | rain) = 0.85 and P(forecast rain | no rain) = 0.20. The forecast predicts rain. What is P(actual rain | forecast rain)?

explanations.B: 0.30 is the prior P(rain) before reading the forecast.
explanations.C: 0.85 is P(forecast rain | rain), the likelihood—not the reversed conditional.
explanations.D: 17/20 confuses the false-alarm rate with the posterior; 0.20 is P(FR | no rain).

remediation.question: Bayes' theorem reverses conditioning: P(A|B) is proportional to:

explanations.A: Correct. Likelihood times prior; divide by P(B) to normalize.
explanations.B: P(A|B) × P(B) = P(A ∩ B), which is not the unnormalized posterior weight.
explanations.C: The prior alone ignores how informative the evidence B is.
explanations.D: P(B) alone is the normalizer, not the numerator.

remediation.question: A rare disease has prevalence 1%. A test has sensitivity 99% and false-positive rate 5%. A patient tests positive. The posterior P(disease | positive) is:

explanations.A: Correct. With a rare disease, most positives are false positives despite good sensitivity—the posterior stays well below 99%.
explanations.B: 99% is sensitivity P(+|disease), not P(disease|+).
explanations.C: 5% is the false-positive rate among healthy people, not the posterior.
explanations.D: 50% would require equal prior odds and balanced evidence, which this setup does not give.

placementQuestions.question: P(A|B) equals P(A ∩ B) divided by:

explanations.A: Dividing by P(A) would give P(B|A), not P(A|B).
explanations.B: Correct. Conditioning renormalizes within the event B.
explanations.C: P(A ∪ B) is unrelated to the definition of conditional probability.
explanations.D: The complement of B is not the denominator for P(A|B).

placementQuestions.question: Given a card is a spade, P(ace | spade) equals:

explanations.A: 1/52 is the unconditional probability of any specific card.
explanations.B: Correct. One ace among 13 spades: 1/13.
explanations.C: 1/4 is P(spade) from the full deck.
explanations.D: 4/13 would count all aces, not aces within spades.

placementQuestions.question: If P(B) = 0.4 and P(A|B) = 0.25, while P(A|Bᶜ) = 0.5, then P(A) equals:

explanations.A: 0.25 is P(A|B) alone, not weighted by scenarios.
explanations.C: 0.35 might come from a weighted average with incorrect weights.
explanations.D: 0.75 exceeds any single conditional probability.

placementQuestions.question: Bayes' theorem converts:
options: P(H|E) into terms involving P(E|H) and P(H)
P(E|H) into P(H|E) without any denominator

explanations.A: Correct. Posterior = likelihood × prior / P(E).
explanations.B: P(H) and P(E) are different quantities; one does not simply become the other.
explanations.C: A denominator P(E) is always required to normalize.
explanations.D: Bayes updates beliefs; it does not force certainty.

placementQuestions.question: A rare disease with a positive test result usually yields a posterior still below 0.5 because:
options: false positives can dominate when the disease is uncommon

explanations.A: Correct. Low base rate × many healthy people = many false positives.
explanations.B: Real tests have positive sensitivity; the issue is base rates.
explanations.C: Disease prevalence is rarely 0.5.
explanations.D: Medical testing is a classic Bayes application.

placementQuestions.question: Coin from Box 1 (P heads = 0.9) with weight 0.2, or Box 2 (P heads = 0.1) with weight 0.8. P(heads) equals:

explanations.A: 0.9 ignores that Box 2 is more likely.
explanations.B: 0.1 is Box 2's rate only.
explanations.D: 0.5 would require symmetric weighting and rates.

explanations.A: Correct. Reversing conditioning changes the denominator.
explanations.B: Intersection is commutative; these are the same event.
explanations.C: Complement notation is equivalent to 1 − P(A).
explanations.D: The empty set always has probability 0.

placementQuestions.question: Two equally likely urns: Urn X has P(red)=0.6, Urn Y has P(red)=0.2. After one red draw, P(Urn X | red) equals:

explanations.A: 0.6 is P(red | X), not the posterior on the urn.
explanations.C: 0.5 is the prior before observing red.
explanations.D: 0.2 is P(red | Y).
